original_abstract: Recent breakthroughs in deep learning-based generative models have rapidly transitioned into the domain of time series analysis, offering remarkable performance gains. However, the efficacy of these deep neural networks remains heavily contingent on the availability of large, consistent datasets—a requirement seldom met in real-world scenarios, where data is often scarce, noisy, or subject to strict constraints. To address these limitations, data augmentation has emerged as a critical strategy, ranging from traditional techniques like noise injection and permutation to the sophisticated synthesis of entirely new data points. This paper provides a systematic review of the current state-of-the-art in time series generation, offering a comprehensive overview of available algorithms and introducing a formal taxonomy of the most significant research. Central to this study is an evaluation of the efficiency of different architectural variants, an analysis of the various metrics used to assess performance, and a discussion of the inherent challenges facing each model. Ultimately, this work aims to synthesize the evolution of the field and highlight the most promising methodologies to serve as a roadmap for future research.
humanized_abstract: Deep learning models such as Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) have gained incredible amount of attention in recent times due to their extraordinary results in time series forecasting. Deep learning models take only few hours to implement while results are available within few minutes to hours. The performance of deep learning models relies heavily on quality of training data. It is not always possible to get large amount of high quality training data. Time series forecasting models such as ARIMA, SARIMA and Prophet require minimum few months training data. Therefore it becomes necessary to increase the amount of available data in order to train models effectively so as to produce optimum results. Data augmentation is a technique that increases amount of available data in training dataset by applying small perturbations to input data. These perturbations can be noise addition, sequence permutations, learnable sequence transformations or generation of new sequences. This survey paper reviews time series generation and models. The characteristics and taxonomies of various time series generation models are discussed. This paper not only compares the efficiencies of different architectural variants, but also analyzes the various evaluation metrics and discusses their challenges and drawbacks. Finally, it summarizes the various models and their contributions to time series generation, acting as a reference guide for further works.
